skill.name: Common RE scripting environments
skill.description: Expert reverse engineer specializing in binary analysis, disassembly, decompilation, and software analysis
skill.summary: Reverse engineering assistant for binary analysis, CTF challenges, and security research
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill providing guidance on reverse engineering workflows. Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 lines, detecting no suspicious patterns and computing a risk score of 0/100. The skill contains only documentation and educational content with explicit ethical guidelines requiring authorized use only.

content.user_title: Analyze binaries and reverse engineer software
content.value_statement: Reverse engineering complex binaries requires expertise in multiple tools and methodologies. This skill provides structured guidance for binary analysis, disassembly, and security research workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guide through systematic binary analysis workflows including reconnaissance, static analysis, and dynamic analysis phases
Explain code patterns like string obfuscation, anti-debugging techniques, and API hashing commonly found in binaries
Recommend appropriate reverse engineering tools such as IDA Pro, Ghidra, radare2, x64dbg, and scripting frameworks
Provide methodology for CTF challenge solving, library analysis, and protocol extraction
Document findings including function signatures, data structures, and algorithm behavior
Apply security and ethical guidelines ensuring analysis is conducted for authorized purposes only
limitations: Cannot directly execute or interact with binary files - provides guidance only
Requires user to have appropriate reverse engineering tools installed locally
Cannot perform actual dynamic analysis or debugging - explains methodologies for users to follow
Analysis depth depends on user providing sufficient context and binary information

use_cases.title: CTF Challenge Solver
use_cases.description: Guides through reverse engineering CTF challenges with systematic approaches for finding passwords, understanding encryption, and bypassing protections
use_cases.target_user: Security competition participants and CTF players

use_cases.title: Security Researcher
use_cases.description: Assists with authorized malware analysis, vulnerability research, and understanding undocumented software behavior for defensive security purposes
use_cases.target_user: Security analysts and defensive security researchers

use_cases.title: Software Interoperability Engineer
use_cases.description: Helps analyze closed-source libraries and APIs to enable legitimate interoperability and integration work
use_cases.target_user: Developers working with proprietary systems and APIs

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Binary Type Identification
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a binary file I need to analyze. What commands should I run first to identify its type, architecture, and basic properties? What information should I look for in the initial triage?
prompt_templates.scenario: First steps in reverse engineering an unknown binary

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Finding the Main Function
prompt_templates.prompt: I loaded a binary into Ghidra and see many functions. How do I identify the entry point and main function? What patterns should I look for?
prompt_templates.scenario: Navigating a disassembled binary in IDA Pro or Ghidra

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: CTF Password Challenge
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a CTF binary that prompts for a password. What analysis approach should I take? Should I focus on static analysis to find string comparisons, or use dynamic analysis with breakpoints? Walk me through the methodology.
prompt_templates.scenario: Solving a password-check CTF reverse engineering challenge

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Anti-Debugging Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: I suspect this binary has anti-debugging protections. What techniques should I look for like IsDebuggerPresent checks, timing checks, or exception-based detection? How do I bypass them safely in a controlled analysis environment?
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyzing malware or protected software with anti-analysis techniques

output_examples.input: I have a CTF binary that asks for a password. How do I approach this?
output: Start with initial reconnaissance using file and checksec commands to identify binary type and security protections
Load the binary in Ghidra or IDA Pro and locate the main function by finding the entry point
Search for string comparison functions like strcmp or memcmp that likely validate the password
Set breakpoints on comparison functions in GDB and examine memory at the comparison point to see the expected value

output_examples.input: How do I identify string obfuscation in a binary?
output: Look for XOR loops with a key byte: for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) str[i] ^= key
Check for stack string construction patterns where strings are built from DWORD assignments
Search for unusual constants that might be XOR keys or rolling cipher parameters
Use Ghidra or IDA strings feature to find decoded strings at runtime

best_practices: Always work in an isolated virtual machine environment when analyzing unknown or potentially malicious binaries
Document findings systematically including function purposes, parameter meanings, and data structure layouts as you analyze
Validate analysis results through multiple approaches combining static disassembly and dynamic debugging
anti_patterns: Running unknown binaries directly on your host system without proper isolation and safety measures
Assuming decompiler output is always correct without verifying against disassembly and runtime behavior
Analyzing binaries without clear authorization which may violate laws or terms of service

faq.question: What tools do I need to get started with reverse engineering?
faq.answer: For beginners, start with Ghidra (free from NSA) or IDA Free for disassembly, x64dbg for dynamic debugging on Windows, and radare2 for command-line analysis. Add GDB with GEF or Pwndbg for Linux binary debugging.

faq.question: Is reverse engineering legal?
faq.answer: Reverse engineering legality depends on jurisdiction and purpose. It is generally legal for security research, interoperability, and CTF competitions with proper authorization. Never analyze software you do not have rights to examine or for unauthorized access purposes.

faq.question: How do I learn to read assembly code?
faq.answer: Start by understanding calling conventions, common instruction patterns, and control flow structures. Practice with simple CTF challenges, follow along with disassembled code while reading source, and use resources like Practical Reverse Engineering or LiveOverflow tutorials.

faq.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic analysis?
faq.answer: Static analysis examines the binary without executing it, using disassemblers and decompilers to understand code structure. Dynamic analysis runs the binary in a controlled environment with debuggers to observe actual behavior, memory changes, and API calls.

faq.question: How do I handle packed or obfuscated binaries?
faq.answer: Use packer detection tools like PEiD or Exeinfo PE first. For unpacking, either find existing unpackers for known packers, or dump the binary from memory after it unpacks itself using tools like Scylla or manual dumping in x64dbg.

faq.question: Can this skill analyze binaries directly?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides guidance and methodology for reverse engineering workflows. You must run analysis tools locally on your own systems. The skill helps you understand what to look for and how to interpret findings from your tools.
